news_summary: Today's positive human rights news highlights meaningful mentorship in academia, strengthened European Union support for global peace efforts, and recognition of LGBTQ+ history through an interactive map in Prague.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles du jour en matière de droits de l'homme mettent en avant un programme de mentorat porteur d'espoir dans le milieu universitaire, le renforcement du soutien de l'Union européenne aux efforts en faveur de la paix mondiale, ainsi que la mise en valeur de l'histoire de la communauté LGBTQ+ grâce à une carte interactive à Prague.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan la importante labor de mentoría en el ámbito académico, el refuerzo del apoyo de la Unión Europea a los esfuerzos por la paz mundial y el reconocimiento de la historia LGBTQ+ a través de un mapa interactivo en Praga.

articles.title: 14 things our PhD supervisors got right and why it mattered
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 13 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00853-wPhD students reflect on how their supervisors made a meaningful difference — from quiet acts of kindness to career-shaping guidance.
articles.content:
Nature’s 2025 PhD survey This article is the fifth in a series discussing the results of Nature’s 2025 PhD survey. The fourth one looked at why PhD students study abroad. The survey, created in partnership with Thinks Insight & Strategy, a research consultancy in London, launched in May and was advertised on nature.com, in Springer Nature digital products and through e-mail campaigns. It had 3,785 self-selecting respondents across 107 countries, with 44% describing themselves as female, 25% as belonging to a minority ethnic group in their country of study and 33% as studying outside their country of origin. The full survey data sets are available at go.nature.com/4ncsuo1.

When someone talks about doing a PhD, the stories that surface are usually about what went wrong: the overbearing adviser, the chaotic laboratory experiments, the loneliness and the stress. But the experience is rarely only that. Amid challenges such as funding uncertainty, competition for positions, pressure to publish and disruptions caused by global conflicts and crises, many supervisors quietly do things that change a student’s trajectory for the better.

In its 2025 PhD survey, Nature asked more than 3,700 doctoral candidates around the world a simple question: what is the best thing your supervisor has done for you during your studies? Their answers offer a counter-narrative to some of the more alarming stories that people sometimes tell about their PhD experiences. Some respondents describe life-changing acts of support. Others recall smaller but no less meaningful moments of trust, generosity, flexibility and protection.

Here, in the students’ own words, and with a few of the supervisors sharing their approaches in response, we highlight several moments when good supervision made all the difference.

Treated me as a colleague

“The best thing my supervisor has done for me is consistently trust me and treat me as a colleague. From early on, I was encouraged to take ownership of my project, make decisions and contribute intellectually beyond the bench. This autonomy was always balanced with support and thoughtful guidance, which helped me grow in confidence and independence. That trust made a real difference — especially in a challenging research and funding environment like Argentina’s, where resilience and creativity are essential.” — Tomas Peters, a PhD candidate studying cell and molecular biology at the University of Buenos Aires

Peters’s supervisor Graciela Boccaccio says: “As a supervisor, my role is to identify and foster new talents. Each member of my team is unique, and I work to create the conditions that allow them to develop their individual scientific skills. I have always considered them young colleagues — perhaps with less experience, but nevertheless creative and wise in many ways.”

Are these the happiest PhD students in the world?

Pushed me to face my fears

“My supervisor pushes me to challenge myself and my fears. He pushed me to apply for talks at conferences, to apply for grants and to step out of my comfort zone. It was extremely uncomfortable and I was stressed, but I’m definitely more confident in my work, abilities and value now because of it. In hindsight, it gave me great experiences and opportunities that I would have turned down otherwise due to being timid.” — Anonymous PhD candidate in Australia

Supported me through my gender transition

“My supervisor is incredibly supportive and has been wonderful throughout my transition. She’s been an ally and advocate even when I haven’t been in the room and understands that there is a mental-health aspect to transitioning (such as fear of discrimination) that can impact your ability to come into the office. She has been incredibly accommodating of changes in my need to work from home.” — Anonymous non-binary PhD candidate in Australia

Backed my decision to have children

“My supervisor fully supported me when I told her that my husband and I wanted to have a child. She gave me a lot of flexibility for my maternity leave and keeps supporting me every time my child gets sick and I need to stay home. Professionally, she keeps reminding me to believe in myself, to push the imposter syndrome away — and I do.” — Laura Lafuente Gracia, a PhD candidate focusing on computational modelling of bone-fracture healing at the Catholic University of Leuven (KU Leuven) in Belgium

Gracia’s supervisor Liesbet Geris says: “When I started as a principal investigator, my PhD students were the age of my younger siblings. Now, the current students are slowly getting closer in age to my children, and that is probably the way that I feel about them. They are my scientific family and I treat them the way I would want my children to be treated. I love figuring out the right way to supervise a student, helping them to find out what it is that they really want out of their career, and making sure they are prepared for that future.”

27 things we wish we’d known when we started our PhDs

Told me that it was OK to struggle

“My supervisor asked me ‘Do you trust me? If you do, then please tell me when you’re struggling, and I will remind you that you are doing a good job and that I’m proud of you.’ It really made me feel that it was OK to struggle, and that if I was struggling, I didn’t need to deal with those negative feelings alone.” — Emmarie Alexander, a PhD candidate studying genetics and genomics at Texas A&M University in College Station

Built up my professional network

“My supervisor is the best part of my PhD. She loves what she does, and her passion for the subject is contagious. She works to create many interdisciplinary connections, both inside and outside the university, for me. These have been incredibly valuable, and I expect them to continue to be so for many years. The collaborations are built based on wherever the project seems to take us. We connect with experts in adjacent fields that our lab may not be well versed in to better our research. And, vice versa, we lend our lab’s expertise to help where we can. I find having connections outside the lab, especially in different disciplines, incredibly helpful for thoroughly understanding a topic.” — Maria Corkran, a PhD candidate studying cell and molecular biology and focusing on virology and host–pathogen interactions at the University of Maryland in College Park

Corkran’s supervisor Margaret Scull says: “I have been very fortunate over my career to work with many phenomenal scientists, and knowing these individuals has not only influenced and elevated my own work but also created opportunities for me that I would not have had otherwise. I am intentional in my efforts to help my trainees build a broad network and interact with investigators who are either using different approaches or working in different roles or research environments. My hope is that, beyond the technical and communication skills gained, my students will have a better sense of their own interests and what they can do with their PhD.”

Listened when it mattered

“My supervisor has consistently kept an open mind and listened to me throughout my studies, which has been the most important quality to help support me through all of the unpredictable and inevitable challenges. I specifically looked for a supervisor who had this quality and approach, and it took me years to find him, but this true support is what helps me find solutions instead of creating more problems and more stress.” — Anonymous PhD candidate in Canada

Offered support during COVID-19

“I began my PhD when the COVID-19 pandemic was in full swing. The situation around us was terrifying; we students were kept physically isolated for many months. During that period, my PhD adviser made sure that he was available to all of his students and offered us emotional support. His office was not only a place where I could brainstorm my science but also a safe space for discussing personal aspects of my life. That really helped me.” — Bharadwaj Vemparala, a PhD candidate focusing on computational biology at the Indian Institute of Science in Bengaluru

My PI yelled at me and I’m devastated. What do I do?

Vemparala’s adviser Narendra Dixit says: “I have found that viewing PhD candidates as young adults, and not just as young researchers, is important. Often, resolving issues outside research is key to their progress. We knew during the pandemic that students would not have access to their families and friends and were thus likely to be in a difficult mental state. We were trying to be as supportive as possible.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the positive, meaningful impact of supportive PhD supervision on students worldwide, including emotional support, career development, and inclusivity (e.g., support during gender transition and parenthood). It presents a broad, significant, and substantive counter-narrative to common negative experiences in academia, benefiting many students globally.
articles.personality_title: How supportive PhD supervisors are making a real difference for students worldwide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many stories about PhD studies focus on difficulties like stress, loneliness, and tough supervisors. However, a large survey by the journal Nature asked over 3,700 PhD students from 107 countries about the best things their supervisors did for them. This revealed many positive experiences that helped students succeed.

**What happened** – The Nature 2025 PhD survey collected students’ reflections on their supervisors’ support during their studies. Respondents shared examples of supervisors treating them as colleagues, encouraging them to overcome fears, supporting life changes like gender transition and parenthood, and offering emotional support during hard times such as the COVID-19 pandemic.

**Impact** – These stories show that good supervision can help students gain confidence, develop their careers, and feel valued. For example, one student said their supervisor trusted them to lead research decisions, while another appreciated encouragement to speak at conferences. Supervisors also helped students balance work and personal life, showing kindness and flexibility. This kind of support is important because PhD studies are often challenging and stressful.

**What’s next step** – Sharing these positive experiences can encourage more supervisors to adopt supportive practices. It may also help universities and research groups understand the value of treating students with respect and care. In the future, this could improve PhD experiences for many more students around the world.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Supportive PhD supervisors who listen, encourage, and care make a meaningful difference in students’ academic and personal lives worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment des directeurs de thèse bienveillants changent la vie des doctorants dans le monde entier
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les récits sur les doctorats parlent souvent de stress, de solitude et de directeurs difficiles. Mais une grande enquête menée par la revue Nature a demandé à plus de 3 700 doctorants dans 107 pays ce que leurs encadrants avaient fait de mieux pour eux. Cela a révélé de nombreuses expériences positives qui ont aidé les étudiants à réussir.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’enquête Nature 2025 a recueilli des témoignages d’étudiants sur le soutien de leurs directeurs durant leurs études. Les réponses montrent des encadrants qui traitent les doctorants comme des collègues, les encouragent à dépasser leurs peurs, soutiennent des changements de vie comme la transition de genre ou la parentalité, et offrent un soutien émotionnel en période difficile, comme pendant la pandémie de COVID-19.

**Impact** – Ces histoires montrent que la bonne supervision aide les étudiants à gagner en confiance, à faire avancer leur carrière et à se sentir valorisés. Par exemple, un étudiant a apprécié la confiance de son directeur pour prendre des décisions de recherche, un autre l’encouragement à parler en conférence. Les encadrants aident aussi à concilier vie professionnelle et personnelle avec gentillesse et souplesse. Ce soutien est important car le doctorat est souvent une période difficile.

**Étape suivante** – Partager ces expériences positives peut encourager plus de directeurs à adopter des pratiques bienveillantes. Cela peut aussi aider les universités à mieux comprendre l’importance de respecter et soutenir leurs doctorants. À l’avenir, cela pourrait améliorer l’expérience des doctorants dans le monde entier.

**En une phrase** – Des directeurs de thèse à l’écoute, encourageants et attentionnés font une vraie différence dans la vie académique et personnelle des doctorants partout dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo los supervisores de doctorado comprensivos están marcando la diferencia para estudiantes en todo el mundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las historias sobre doctorados suelen centrarse en el estrés, la soledad y supervisores difíciles. Pero una gran encuesta realizada por la revista Nature preguntó a más de 3.700 estudiantes de doctorado de 107 países qué fue lo mejor que hicieron sus supervisores. Esto mostró muchas experiencias positivas que ayudaron a los estudiantes a tener éxito.

**Qué pasó** – La encuesta PhD 2025 de Nature reunió testimonios sobre el apoyo de los supervisores durante los estudios. Las respuestas incluyen supervisores que tratan a los estudiantes como colegas, los animan a enfrentar sus miedos, apoyan cambios de vida como la transición de género y la maternidad/paternidad, y ofrecen apoyo emocional en momentos difíciles, como la pandemia de COVID-19.

**Impacto** – Estas historias muestran que una buena supervisión ayuda a los estudiantes a ganar confianza, avanzar en su carrera y sentirse valorados. Por ejemplo, un estudiante valoró que su supervisor confiara en él para tomar decisiones, otro agradeció el impulso para hablar en conferencias. Los supervisores también ayudan a equilibrar la vida personal y académica con amabilidad y flexibilidad. Este apoyo es importante porque el doctorado es un proceso exigente.

**Próximo paso** – Compartir estas experiencias positivas puede inspirar a más supervisores a adoptar prácticas comprensivas. También puede ayudar a universidades a entender mejor la importancia de respetar y apoyar a sus estudiantes. Esto podría mejorar la experiencia de muchos estudiantes de doctorado en el futuro.

**Una frase clave** – Supervisores de doctorado que escuchan, animan y cuidan marcan una diferencia real en la vida académica y personal de los estudiantes en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse group of glowing lanterns floating gently above an open book, each lantern connected by delicate threads symbolizing trust, support, and growth, set against a soft, muted background of a quiet study space with scattered scientific instruments and a nurturing hand reaching out to steady one lantern.

articles.title: European Union support for the UN ‘more vital than ever’, Security Council hears
articles.summary: A senior United Nations official on Monday hailed the European Union (EU) as “a major economic and diplomatic actor and a strong advocate of multilateralism.”
articles.content:
UN Assistant Secretary-General for Political Affairs Khaled Khiari was speaking during the Security Council’s annual debate on cooperation between the two organizations in the areas of peace and security.

“In an increasingly complex and uncertain international environment, sustained engagement, dialogue and trust-building are essential,” he said.

“Respect for international law and the Charter of the United Nations remains central to addressing shared challenges. The European Union plays an important role in advancing these principles.”

Partner in peace and beyond

Mr. Khiari said the EU is a key partner across the full spectrum of the UN’s work, from international peace and security to human rights, sustainable development and humanitarian action.

He expressed particular gratitude for the role of its member states as troop and police-contributing countries, including in Lebanon and other challenging environments.

“At a time when advancing political solutions remains difficult in many contexts, the support of the European Union to the United Nations’ good offices, mediation and political engagement is especially important,” he added.

Hopes for Ukraine ceasefire

Mr. Khiari said the war in Ukraine “is a severe test to the international order-related regional frameworks” and that Russia’s invasion of the country “has shaken the foundations of the European security architecture to its core.”

With the conflict now entering a fifth year, and the human toll continuing to rise, he expressed hope that the Orthodox Easter temporary ceasefire will help pave the way towards a more durable truce.

“An immediate, full and unconditional ceasefire must be the first step towards a just, lasting and comprehensive peace, grounded in respect for international law and the principles of the United Nations Charter,” he said.

UN Photo/Manuel Elías Kaja Kallas, High Representative of the European Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy, briefs the Security Council meeting on cooperation between the UN and European Union.

EU support

Kaja Kallas, EU High Representative for Foreign Affairs, noted that although the bloc’s member states comprise only five per cent of the global population, they provide 42 per cent of Official Development Assistance (ODA) and finance a quarter of the UN’s regular budget.

“We pay in full and on time,” she said. “But the EU has never considered our support for the United Nations purely on how much we spend.”

The EU supports the UN “because it is the only international system that stands for peace, represents the global collective interest, and protects international law,” she explained.

“Today we are witnessing the gravest breakdown of international law since the Second World War,” endangering peace and security. “This is why the EU’s support for the UN now is more vital than ever.”

International law breaking down

Ms. Kallas said the breakdown of international law is evident in Russia’s war of aggression against Ukraine and the war in the Middle East, which she called the two preeminent global crises today.

“Russia is executing what is arguably among the most outrageous breaches of international law in United Nations history by invading a sovereign country and turning its back on its obligations as a Permanent Member of the UN Security Council,” she said.

Meanwhile, “the war in the Middle East has caused immense instability and cost far too many lives,” with ripple effects felt worldwide. People from Europe to Asia are suffering from higher energy prices, and farmers from Sudan to South America are facing fertiliser shortages.

“Russia is also supporting Iran with intelligence and drones. Prolonging the war benefits Russia through higher energy prices, depleted air defence arsenals and diverted attention,” she said.

New world order

These two crises “represent the clearest sign yet of an abandonment of the old rules, including the UN Charter,” she continued.

“A new world is now in the making, characterised by competition and coercive power politics; a world order dominated by a handful of military powers who aim to establish spheres of influence.”

She reminded the Council that “we have been here before”, highlighting how the international community chose multilateralism over multipolarity in the aftermath of the First and Second World Wars.

She stressed that “Europe will always choose cooperation over coercion.”

Responsibility to reform

Ms. Kallas said that in 100 years’ time, “historians will look back at this moment. And they will ask: What did the United Nations do when the rules-based order was under threat?

“Did we cling to the past, defending a system that no longer worked for the many? Or did we have the courage to reform it – to make it stronger, fairer, and more resilient?”

She insisted that “the answer is in our hands” and concluded with a message for Council members.

“You are entrusted with a responsibility to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war. That was the promise of 1945, and it must be our mission today,” she said.

“Let us be the generation that built a stronger, fairer order, one fit for the challenges ahead. The time for action is now.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the European Union's vital support for the United Nations in promoting peace, security, human rights, and international law during critical global crises, including the war in Ukraine and conflicts in the Middle East. It emphasizes multilateral cooperation, the importance of upholding international law, and the call for reforming the UN system to build a stronger, fairer global order. The story has broad significance for global peace and security and contains substantial context on the EU-UN partnership and its impact.
articles.personality_title: EU’s vital support strengthens UN efforts for peace and security
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The United Nations (UN) and the European Union (EU) work together to promote peace, security, human rights, and development around the world. This partnership is especially important during global crises like the war in Ukraine and conflicts in the Middle East.

**What happened** – At a recent UN Security Council meeting, senior officials praised the EU for being a key partner in supporting the UN’s work. The EU contributes troops, police, funding, and political support, helping the UN carry out peacekeeping, mediation, and humanitarian efforts. The EU also provides a large share of the UN’s budget and development aid.

**Impact** – The EU’s backing is crucial because it helps the UN uphold international law and respond to major conflicts that threaten global stability. For example, the EU is pushing for a ceasefire in Ukraine and calls for reforms to make the UN stronger and fairer. This support shows how countries can work together to solve difficult problems.

**What’s next step** – The UN and EU will continue cooperating to seek peaceful solutions and reform the UN system. Leaders hope that current ceasefires lead to lasting peace and that the UN can adapt to new global challenges by improving its rules and operations.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Union’s strong support helps the United Nations promote peace and security during critical global challenges and encourages reforms for a better international system.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le soutien vital de l’UE renforce les efforts de l’ONU pour la paix et la sécurité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Nations Unies (ONU) et l’Union européenne (UE) collaborent pour promouvoir la paix, la sécurité, les droits de l’homme et le développement dans le monde. Ce partenariat est particulièrement important lors de crises mondiales comme la guerre en Ukraine et les conflits au Moyen-Orient.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Lors d’une récente réunion du Conseil de sécurité de l’ONU, des responsables ont salué l’UE comme un partenaire clé soutenant le travail de l’ONU. L’UE fournit des troupes, des policiers, des financements et un soutien politique, aidant l’ONU à mener des opérations de maintien de la paix, de médiation et d’aide humanitaire. L’UE finance aussi une grande partie du budget et de l’aide au développement de l’ONU.

**Impact** – Le soutien de l’UE est crucial car il aide l’ONU à faire respecter le droit international et à répondre aux conflits majeurs qui menacent la stabilité mondiale. Par exemple, l’UE pousse pour un cessez-le-feu en Ukraine et appelle à réformer l’ONU pour la rendre plus forte et plus juste. Ce soutien montre comment les pays peuvent travailler ensemble pour résoudre des problèmes difficiles.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU et l’UE continueront de coopérer pour trouver des solutions pacifiques et réformer le système de l’ONU. Les dirigeants espèrent que les cessez-le-feu actuels mèneront à une paix durable et que l’ONU pourra s’adapter aux nouveaux défis mondiaux en améliorant ses règles et son fonctionnement.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Le fort soutien de l’Union européenne aide l’ONU à promouvoir la paix et la sécurité lors de défis mondiaux majeurs et encourage des réformes pour un meilleur système international.
articles.personality_title_es: El apoyo vital de la UE fortalece los esfuerzos de la ONU por la paz y seguridad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las Naciones Unidas (ONU) y la Unión Europea (UE) trabajan juntas para promover la paz, la seguridad, los derechos humanos y el desarrollo en todo el mundo. Esta colaboración es especialmente importante durante crisis globales como la guerra en Ucrania y los conflictos en Medio Oriente.

**Qué pasó** – En una reciente reunión del Consejo de Seguridad de la ONU, altos funcionarios elogiaron a la UE como un socio clave que apoya el trabajo de la ONU. La UE aporta tropas, policías, financiamiento y apoyo político, ayudando a la ONU a realizar operaciones de mantenimiento de la paz, mediación y ayuda humanitaria. También financia gran parte del presupuesto y la ayuda al desarrollo de la ONU.

**Impacto** – El respaldo de la UE es fundamental porque ayuda a la ONU a defender el derecho internacional y a responder a conflictos importantes que amenazan la estabilidad mundial. Por ejemplo, la UE impulsa un alto el fuego en Ucrania y llama a reformar la ONU para hacerla más fuerte y justa. Este apoyo muestra cómo los países pueden colaborar para resolver problemas difíciles.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU y la UE seguirán cooperando para buscar soluciones pacíficas y reformar el sistema de la ONU. Los líderes esperan que los actuales altos el fuego conduzcan a una paz duradera y que la ONU pueda adaptarse a nuevos retos globales mejorando sus reglas y funcionamiento.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El fuerte apoyo de la Unión Europea ayuda a la ONU a promover la paz y seguridad durante desafíos globales críticos y fomenta reformas para un mejor sistema internacional.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two intertwined olive branches—one adorned with blue stars symbolizing the European Union, the other bearing a stylized white dove representing the United Nations—set against a softly glowing globe with faint outlines of Ukraine and the Middle East, surrounded by gentle rays of light symbolizing peace, cooperation, and hope amid complex global challenges.

articles.title: Interactive map honors LGBTQ+ people’s historic presence in Prague
articles.summary: As shown in an interactive map of Prague, listing historical figures and places associated with this community, LGBTQ+ people have always been part of Czech culture.
articles.content:
In the Czech Republic, the debate on LGBTQ+ people’s rights oscillates between a tolerant rhetoric towards this community and exploitation by predominantly right-wing politicians, who consider the gender issue as something foreign, if not a violation of local traditions. However, as shown in an interactive map of Prague, listing historical figures and places associated with this community, LGBTQ+ people have always been part of Czech culture.

Homophobic rhetoric under the pretext of political protectionism

In Central and Eastern Europe, which remains more marred by homophobia than Western Europe, where LGBTQ+ activism has been changing attitudes and laws since the 1980s, the Czech Republic is considered a comparatively more tolerant country. Progressive narratives on gender issues in the inter-war period, and one of Europe’s lowest levels of religiosity (about 20 percent of believers ), may explain why.

But this is not to say Czech LGBTQ+ people are protected equally by law or on the streets. Although homosexual relationships, condemned during the communist era, were decriminalized in 1961 and registered partnerships were passed in 2006, parliament has refused to ratify the marriage bill for same-sex couples, despite positive changes to inheritance rights in 2024. That said, almost two-thirds of Czechs support the bill’s approval.



However, according to a study conducted in 2024, more than 40 percent of Czechs belonging to this community report instances of verbal or physical abuse. In more than 90 percent of cases, victims do not file complaints.

The most serious homophobia probably comes from politicians. They don't hesitate in making public homophobic statements in a country that is a member of the European Union and, therefore, bound to anti-discrimination legislation under Article 21 of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights.

As reported in this Global Voices article, leading figures, such as former president Miloš Zeman, former prime minister Petr Fiala, and Senator and presidential candidate Pavel Fischer, have made public discriminatory remarks without any consequences. They often present gender diversity as a “cultural import” from Brussels or the United States.



A recent incident emphasized the culture of impunity toward some politicians. Minister of Foreign Affairs Petr Macinka from the far-right party Motorists for Themselves, who attended the Munich Security Conference in February 2026, clashed with former U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton on gender. He said:

There is male, and female, and the rest, probably, is a social construct.This [the issue of gender] is something that went too far.

The medieval roots of Prague’s queer scene

It was in light of this disregard that the Czech LGBTQ+ activist group and NGO, Společnost Pro Queer Pamět (Society for Queer Memory), launched an initiative, educating citizens about the ancient, vibrant history of LGBTQ+ people’s presence in Czech culture.



In February 2022, the NGO launched an online interactive map of Prague, called Queer Prague, listing places where LGBTQ+ people lived and worked, as well as locations with monuments associated with this community. This guide, first published in print in 2014, features 160 points that, when clicked, open information pages explaining the cultural and historical context of LGBTQ+ people or places.

The famous Republic Square, Náměstí Republiky, once named Josef Square during the Austro-Hungarian Empire until 1918, in the historic center of the city, was known as a “cruising area,” a public meeting place for gay men:

Ladislav Jackson, a representative for the NGO, explained the project’s origin and development in an interview with Global Voices:

Všechno začalo v roce 2009, kdy se konala vůbec první mezioborová konference Homosexualita v české vědě, kde se setkalo významné množství historiků, historiček, historiků a historiček kultury, umění a literatury. Výsledkem byly tři akademické knihy, které pak historik Jan Seidl navrhl přetvořit v praktický průvodce po Praze s názvem Teplá Praha (anglicky Queer Prague). Kniha se záhy vyprodala, tak jsme přistoupili k vytvoření on-line verze. Místa jsou tedy jakákoli místa, o kterých víme, že byla nějakým způsobem spojena s minulostí queer osob, historií queer emancipace a queer kultury. Nejstarší místo je z roku 1376, kdy je zaznamenán vůbec první soudní případ homosexuálního chování, šlo ale o násilné, zavrženíhodné akty. Pak je tam několik příběhů z dob novověku, většina událostí je ale z první poloviny 20. století. It all started in 2009, at the first Interdisciplinary Conference on Homosexuality in Czech Science, which brought together historians of art, culture, and literature. It resulted in three academic works, which the historian Jan Seidl suggested transforming into a practical guide for Prague titled ‘Queer Prague.’ As the book quickly flew off the shelves, we created an online version. The places mentioned are all those that we know are somehow associated with LGBTQ+ people’s history, their emancipation, and queer culture. The oldest place dates back to 1376, the year of the first recorded homosexuality trial, involving violent and reprehensible acts. Then there are modern-day stories, but most of the events happened in the first half of the 20th century.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful initiative by a Czech LGBTQ+ NGO that created an interactive map documenting the historic presence and contributions of LGBTQ+ people in Prague. This project promotes awareness, cultural recognition, and counters homophobic rhetoric in a region where LGBTQ+ rights face political challenges and social discrimination. The story has broad societal significance as it fosters inclusion, education, and historical acknowledgment of marginalized communities.
articles.personality_title: Interactive map reveals Prague’s long LGBTQ+ history
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the Czech Republic, LGBTQ+ rights face mixed attitudes. While the country is more tolerant than some neighbors, many LGBTQ+ people still experience discrimination and political leaders sometimes speak against gender diversity. 

**What happened** – A Czech LGBTQ+ group called Společnost Pro Queer Pamět (Society for Queer Memory) created an interactive online map called Queer Prague. Launched in 2022, it shows 160 places in Prague linked to LGBTQ+ history, including where people lived, worked, or met. The map traces this history back to 1376, highlighting Prague’s long connection to queer culture.

**Impact** – This map helps people learn that LGBTQ+ people have been part of Czech culture for centuries, challenging the idea that gender diversity is a new or foreign concept. It provides a positive way to remember and respect this community’s history, especially in a region where political and social discrimination still exist. 

**What’s next step** – The map is available online for anyone to explore, and the NGO hopes it will encourage more education and acceptance. It may inspire other cities or groups to create similar projects that honor hidden histories and promote understanding.

**One-sentence takeaway** – An interactive map in Prague uncovers centuries of LGBTQ+ history, helping people recognize and respect this community’s lasting presence in Czech culture.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une carte interactive révèle l’histoire LGBTQ+ de Prague
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En République tchèque, les droits des personnes LGBTQ+ suscitent des attitudes variées. Bien que le pays soit plus tolérant que certains voisins, beaucoup de personnes LGBTQ+ subissent encore des discriminations, et certains dirigeants politiques s’opposent à la diversité de genre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un groupe LGBTQ+ tchèque nommé Společnost Pro Queer Pamět (Société pour la Mémoire Queer) a créé une carte interactive en ligne appelée Queer Prague. Lancée en 2022, elle présente 160 lieux à Prague liés à l’histoire LGBTQ+, comme des endroits où des personnes ont vécu, travaillé ou se sont rencontrées. La carte remonte à 1376, montrant la longue relation de Prague avec la culture queer.

**Impact** – Cette carte aide à comprendre que les personnes LGBTQ+ font partie de la culture tchèque depuis des siècles, ce qui contredit l’idée que la diversité de genre serait un concept nouveau ou étranger. Elle offre une façon positive de se souvenir et de respecter l’histoire de cette communauté, surtout dans une région où la discrimination persiste.

**Prochaines étapes** – La carte est disponible en ligne pour que tout le monde puisse la découvrir. L’ONG espère qu’elle encouragera plus d’éducation et d’acceptation. Cela pourrait inspirer d’autres villes ou groupes à créer des projets similaires pour honorer des histoires méconnues et promouvoir la compréhension.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une carte interactive à Prague révèle des siècles d’histoire LGBTQ+, aidant à reconnaître et respecter la présence durable de cette communauté dans la culture tchèque.
articles.personality_title_es: Mapa interactivo revela la larga historia LGBTQ+ de Praga
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la República Checa, los derechos LGBTQ+ enfrentan actitudes mixtas. Aunque el país es más tolerante que algunos vecinos, muchas personas LGBTQ+ aún sufren discriminación y algunos líderes políticos se oponen a la diversidad de género.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo LGBTQ+ checo llamado Společnost Pro Queer Pamět (Sociedad para la Memoria Queer) creó un mapa interactivo en línea llamado Queer Prague. Lanzado en 2022, muestra 160 lugares en Praga relacionados con la historia LGBTQ+, incluidos sitios donde vivieron, trabajaron o se reunieron personas. El mapa data de 1376, mostrando la larga conexión de Praga con la cultura queer.

**Impacto** – Este mapa ayuda a las personas a entender que la comunidad LGBTQ+ ha sido parte de la cultura checa durante siglos, desafiando la idea de que la diversidad de género es algo nuevo o extranjero. Ofrece una manera positiva de recordar y respetar la historia de esta comunidad, especialmente en una región donde aún existen discriminación política y social.

**Próximo paso** – El mapa está disponible en línea para que cualquiera lo explore. La ONG espera que fomente más educación y aceptación. Podría inspirar a otras ciudades o grupos a crear proyectos similares que honren historias ocultas y promuevan la comprensión.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un mapa interactivo en Praga descubre siglos de historia LGBTQ+, ayudando a reconocer y respetar la presencia duradera de esta comunidad en la cultura checa.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a historic Prague cityscape at twilight featuring softly glowing, colorful map markers illuminating key landmarks linked to LGBTQ+ history, with subtle silhouettes of intertwined rainbow-hued ribbons weaving around medieval and modern architectural elements, symbolizing the enduring presence and cultural legacy of queer people in the city.
